title: 2013 NFL season

infobox.value: [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey")

was the 94th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 48th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. The season saw the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season") capture the first championship in the franchise's 38 years in the league with a lopsided victory over the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") in [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII"), the league's championship game. The Super Bowl was played at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), on Sunday, February 2, 2014. It was the first Super Bowl hosted by New Jersey and the first to be held outdoors in a cold weather environment. The Seahawks scored 12 seconds into the game and led the rest of the way on the back of their [Legion of Boom](/wiki/Legion_of_Boom_(Seattle_Seahawks) "Legion of Boom (Seattle Seahawks)") defense. The Seahawks ultimately defeated the Broncos 43–8.

The **2013 NFL season** was the 94th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 48th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. The season saw the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season") capture the first championship in the franchise's 38 years in the league with a lopsided victory over the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") in [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII"), the league's championship game. The Super Bowl was played at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), on Sunday, February 2, 2014. It was the first Super Bowl hosted by New Jersey and the first to be held outdoors in a cold weather environment. The Seahawks scored 12 seconds into the game and led the rest of the way on the back of their [Legion of Boom](/wiki/Legion_of_Boom_(Seattle_Seahawks) "Legion of Boom (Seattle Seahawks)") defense. The Seahawks ultimately defeated the Broncos 43–8.

Broncos quarterback [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") was named the regular season's [Most Valuable Player](/wiki/National_Football_League_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "National Football League Most Valuable Player Award") (MVP) by the voters of the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") (AP) for a record fifth time after compiling passing stats including regular-season records for passing yards and passing touchdowns. Manning also was named the [Offensive Player of the Year](/wiki/AP_NFL_Offensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award "AP NFL Offensive Player of the Year Award") for the second time in his career. [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2013_Carolina_Panthers_season "2013 Carolina Panthers season") linebacker [Luke Kuechly](/wiki/Luke_Kuechly "Luke Kuechly") earned [Defensive Player of the Year](/wiki/National_Football_League_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award#Associated_Press "National Football League Defensive Player of the Year Award") honors.

Scoring reached historic levels throughout the league in 2013. As a whole, the league set records for total points scored, points scored per game, and touchdowns and field goals scored. The Broncos set a new standard for team scoring in the regular season with 606 points. Ten other teams each scored over 400 points, the greatest number to surpass that benchmark in a single year until [2020](/wiki/2020_NFL_season "2020 NFL season").

The [regular season](/wiki/Regular_season_(NFL) "Regular season (NFL)") got underway on Thursday, September 5, 2013, with the Broncos hosting the defending [Super Bowl XLVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII") champion [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season") in the annual [kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"). The game presaged the Broncos' historic offensive production with a strong performance by Peyton Manning in which he tied a league record in throwing seven touchdown passes and led the Broncos to a win. The game was the start of a disappointing season for the Ravens in which they would finish out of the playoffs with an 8–8 record, thus ensuring there would be no repeat Super Bowl winner for a record-tying ninth straight season. The regular season wrapped up on Sunday night, December 29.

The [playoffs](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NFL_playoffs "2013–14 NFL playoffs") began with the wild card round which took place the first weekend of January 2014. The league's propensity for scoring did not abate in the post-season, as exemplified by the [Indianapolis Colts'](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") wild come-from-behind [victory](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NFL_playoffs#AFC:_Indianapolis_Colts_45,_Kansas_City_Chiefs_44 "2013–14 NFL playoffs") over the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") in the playoffs' opening game. The [Conference Championship](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NFL_playoffs#Conference_Championships "2013–14 NFL playoffs") games featured the top seeded teams in each conference, the Seahawks in the NFC and the Broncos in the [American Football Conference](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") (AFC), hosting the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") and [New England Patriots](/wiki/2013_New_England_Patriots_season "2013 New England Patriots season") respectively. Both home teams prevailed to set up just the second Super Bowl matchup of No. 1 seeds in the past 20 seasons.

## Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Player movement")]

The 2013 league year began at 4 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") on March 12,[[1]](#cite_note-2013Calendar-1) which marked the start of the league's [free agency](/wiki/National_Football_League#Free_agency "National Football League") period.[[2]](#cite_note-2013FreeAgentList-2) The per-team [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap#National_Football_League "Salary cap") was set at [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")123,000,000.[[3]](#cite_note-3) For the first time the league instituted a negotiating period prior to the start of free agency during which time [agents](/wiki/Sports_agents "Sports agents") representing prospective unrestricted free agent players (though not the players themselves) were allowed to have contact with team representatives with the purpose of determining a player's market value and to begin contract negotiations. This period, which was referred to by some as the "legal [tampering](/wiki/Tampering_(sport) "Tampering (sport)")" period, began at midnight on March 9.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Free agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Free agency")]

A total of 524 players were eligible for some form of free agency. Among the high-profile players who changed teams via free agency included:

* Quarterbacks [Matt Cassel](/wiki/Matt_Cassel "Matt Cassel") (Kansas City to Minnesota), [Ryan Fitzpatrick](/wiki/Ryan_Fitzpatrick "Ryan Fitzpatrick") (Buffalo to Tennessee), [Matt Hasselbeck](/wiki/Matt_Hasselbeck "Matt Hasselbeck") (Tennessee to Indianapolis), [Brian Hoyer](/wiki/Brian_Hoyer "Brian Hoyer") (Arizona to Cleveland) and [Kevin Kolb](/wiki/Kevin_Kolb "Kevin Kolb") (Arizona to Buffalo)
* Running backs [Ahmad Bradshaw](/wiki/Ahmad_Bradshaw "Ahmad Bradshaw") (New York Giants to Indianapolis), [Reggie Bush](/wiki/Reggie_Bush "Reggie Bush") (Miami to Detroit), [Shonn Greene](/wiki/Shonn_Greene "Shonn Greene") (New York Jets to Tennessee), [Steven Jackson](/wiki/Steven_Jackson "Steven Jackson") (St. Louis to Atlanta) and [Rashard Mendenhall](/wiki/Rashard_Mendenhall "Rashard Mendenhall") (Pittsburgh to Arizona)
* Wide receivers [Danny Amendola](/wiki/Danny_Amendola "Danny Amendola") (St. Louis to New England), [Darrius Heyward-Bey](/wiki/Darrius_Heyward-Bey "Darrius Heyward-Bey") (Oakland to Indianapolis), [Greg Jennings](/wiki/Greg_Jennings "Greg Jennings") (Green Bay to Minnesota), [Mike Wallace](/wiki/Mike_Wallace_(American_football) "Mike Wallace (American football)") (Pittsburgh to Miami) and [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker") (New England to Denver)
* Tight ends [Martellus Bennett](/wiki/Martellus_Bennett "Martellus Bennett") (New York Giants to Chicago), [Jared Cook](/wiki/Jared_Cook "Jared Cook") (Tennessee to St. Louis), [Dustin Keller](/wiki/Dustin_Keller "Dustin Keller") (New York Jets to Miami) and [Delanie Walker](/wiki/Delanie_Walker "Delanie Walker") (San Francisco to Tennessee)
* Offensive tackles [Jermon Bushrod](/wiki/Jermon_Bushrod "Jermon Bushrod") (New Orleans to Chicago), [Gosder Cherilus](/wiki/Gosder_Cherilus "Gosder Cherilus") (Detroit to Indianapolis) and [Jake Long](/wiki/Jake_Long "Jake Long") (Miami to St. Louis)
* Guards [Andy Levitre](/wiki/Andy_Levitre "Andy Levitre") (Buffalo to Tennessee) and [Louis Vasquez](/wiki/Louis_Vasquez_(American_football) "Louis Vasquez (American football)") (Denver to San Diego)
* Defensive ends [Cliff Avril](/wiki/Cliff_Avril "Cliff Avril") (Detroit to Seattle), [Michael Bennett](/wiki/Michael_Bennett_(defensive_lineman,_born_1985) "Michael Bennett (defensive lineman, born 1985)") (Tampa Bay to Seattle), [Elvis Dumervil](/wiki/Elvis_Dumervil "Elvis Dumervil") (Denver to Baltimore), [Dwight Freeney](/wiki/Dwight_Freeney "Dwight Freeney") (Indianapolis to San Diego) and [Osi Umenyiora](/wiki/Osi_Umenyiora "Osi Umenyiora") (New York Giants to Atlanta)
* Defensive tackles [Desmond Bryant](/wiki/Desmond_Bryant "Desmond Bryant") (Oakland to Cleveland), [Ricky Jean-Francois](/wiki/Ricky_Jean-Francois "Ricky Jean-Francois") (San Francisco to Indianapolis), [Jason Jones](/wiki/Jason_Jones_(defensive_end) "Jason Jones (defensive end)") (Seattle to Detroit) and [Sammie Lee Hill](/wiki/Sammie_Lee_Hill "Sammie Lee Hill") (Detroit to Tennessee)
* Linebackers [Connor Barwin](/wiki/Connor_Barwin "Connor Barwin") (Houston to Philadelphia), [James Harrison](/wiki/James_Harrison_(American_football) "James Harrison (American football)") (Pittsburgh to Cincinnati), [Paul Kruger](/wiki/Paul_Kruger_(American_football) "Paul Kruger (American football)") (Baltimore to Cleveland) and [Philip Wheeler](/wiki/Philip_Wheeler "Philip Wheeler") (Oakland to Miami)
* Cornerbacks [Brent Grimes](/wiki/Brent_Grimes "Brent Grimes") (Atlanta to Miami), [Keenan Lewis](/wiki/Keenan_Lewis "Keenan Lewis") (Pittsburgh to New Orleans), [Dunta Robinson](/wiki/Dunta_Robinson "Dunta Robinson") (Atlanta to Kansas City), [Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie](/wiki/Dominique_Rodgers-Cromartie "Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie") (Arizona to Denver) and [Sean Smith](/wiki/Sean_Smith_(cornerback) "Sean Smith (cornerback)") (Miami to Kansas City)
* Safeties [Patrick Chung](/wiki/Patrick_Chung "Patrick Chung") (New England to Philadelphia), [Dashon Goldson](/wiki/Dashon_Goldson "Dashon Goldson") (San Francisco to Tampa Bay), [LaRon Landry](/wiki/LaRon_Landry "LaRon Landry") (New York Jets to Indianapolis) and [Glover Quin](/wiki/Glover_Quin "Glover Quin") (Houston to Detroit).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Eight players were assigned the [non-exclusive franchise tag](/wiki/Franchise_tag#Types_of_franchise_tag "Franchise tag") by their teams, which ensured that the team would receive compensation were the player to sign a contract with another team. These players were [Brandon Albert](/wiki/Brandon_Albert "Brandon Albert") ([Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season")), [Jairus Byrd](/wiki/Jairus_Byrd "Jairus Byrd") ([Bills](/wiki/2013_Buffalo_Bills_season "2013 Buffalo Bills season")), [Ryan Clady](/wiki/Ryan_Clady "Ryan Clady") ([Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season")), [Michael Johnson](/wiki/Michael_Johnson_(defensive_end) "Michael Johnson (defensive end)") ([Bengals](/wiki/2013_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2013 Cincinnati Bengals season")), [Pat McAfee](/wiki/Pat_McAfee "Pat McAfee") ([Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season")), [Henry Melton](/wiki/Henry_Melton "Henry Melton") ([Bears](/wiki/2013_Chicago_Bears_season "2013 Chicago Bears season")), [Anthony Spencer](/wiki/Anthony_E._Spencer_II "Anthony E. Spencer II") ([Cowboys](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season")) and [Randy Starks](/wiki/Randy_Starks "Randy Starks") ([Dolphins](/wiki/2013_Miami_Dolphins_season "2013 Miami Dolphins season")).[[6]](#cite_note-FranchisePlayers-6) None of these players changed teams.

### Major trades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major trades")]

The following trades are notable as they involved [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl")-caliber players and/or draft picks in the first three rounds:

Offseason

* *February 27* – The [Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") acquired quarterback [Alex Smith](/wiki/Alex_Smith "Alex Smith") from the [49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") for the Chiefs' second-round pick in the 2013 draft, [34th overall](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft#Pick_34 "2013 NFL draft") (which the 49ers later traded to the [Titans](/wiki/2013_Tennessee_Titans_season "2013 Tennessee Titans season")) and a conditional pick in the 2014 draft (which turned out to be another second-rounder, [#56 overall](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_56 "2014 NFL draft")).[[7]](#cite_note-AlexSmithTrade-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Smith had been the first overall selection of the [2005 NFL draft](/wiki/2005_NFL_draft "2005 NFL draft"), but had been supplanted as the 49ers starting quarterback in mid-[2012](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2012 San Francisco 49ers season") by [Colin Kaepernick](/wiki/Colin_Kaepernick "Colin Kaepernick").[[7]](#cite_note-AlexSmithTrade-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Percy_Harvin_%28cropped%29.jpg/120px-Percy_Harvin_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Percy_Harvin_(cropped).jpg)

[Percy Harvin](/wiki/Percy_Harvin "Percy Harvin") was traded by the Vikings to the Seahawks

* *March 11* – Wide receiver [Percy Harvin](/wiki/Percy_Harvin "Percy Harvin") was traded by the [Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") to the [Seahawks](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season") for the Seahawks' 2013 first-round ([#25 overall](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft#Pick_25 "2013 NFL draft"), with which the Vikings selected [Xavier Rhodes](/wiki/Xavier_Rhodes "Xavier Rhodes")) and seventh-round ([pick No. 214](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft#Pick_214 "2013 NFL draft")) selections as well as the Seahawks' third-round pick in 2014 ([the 96th selection](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_96 "2014 NFL draft")).[[9]](#cite_note-HarvinTrade-9) Harvin is an [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro") and former [Offensive Rookie of the Year](/wiki/National_Football_League_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award#Offensive_ROTY_winners_(1967–present) "National Football League Rookie of the Year Award"), but he has also suffered a series of injuries throughout his career and had become disgruntled with the Vikings to the point that he asked the team to trade him.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Seahawks subsequently signed Harvin to a 6-year, $67 million contract extension which includes $25.5 million in guaranteed money.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* *March 11* – The [49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") acquired wide receiver [Anquan Boldin](/wiki/Anquan_Boldin "Anquan Boldin") from the [Ravens](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season") for a sixth-round selection in the 2013 draft ([the 199th overall pick](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft#Pick_199 "2013 NFL draft")). Boldin, a three-time [Pro Bowler](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") and former [Offensive Rookie of the Year](/wiki/National_Football_League_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award#Offensive_ROTY_winners_(1967–present) "National Football League Rookie of the Year Award"), had refused to accept a pay cut that the Ravens had requested.[[12]](#cite_note-BoldinTrade-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Darrelle_Revis_ESPNWeekend2010-051.jpg/120px-Darrelle_Revis_ESPNWeekend2010-051.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darrelle_Revis_ESPNWeekend2010-051.jpg)

The Jets traded [Darrelle Revis](/wiki/Darrelle_Revis "Darrelle Revis") to the Buccaneers

* *April 21* – Cornerback [Darrelle Revis](/wiki/Darrelle_Revis "Darrelle Revis") was traded by the [Jets](/wiki/2013_New_York_Jets_season "2013 New York Jets season") to the [Buccaneers](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season").[[13]](#cite_note-RevisTrade-13) The Jets received the Bucs' first round draft pick in 2013, the [13th overall selection](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft#Pick_13 "2013 NFL draft") (which the Jets used to select [Sheldon Richardson](/wiki/Sheldon_Richardson "Sheldon Richardson")) and a conditional pick which would become the Bucs' fourth-round selection in 2014, [the 104th overall pick](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_104 "2014 NFL draft").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Revis, a three time [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro"), was widely considered to be among the league's top defensive players, but he was coming off a knee injury and the Jets did not feel they would be able to retain him after the 2013 season. The Bucs signed Revis to a 6-year, $96 million contract.[[13]](#cite_note-RevisTrade-13)

In-season

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Trent_Richardson.jpg/120px-Trent_Richardson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trent_Richardson.jpg)

[Trent Richardson](/wiki/Trent_Richardson "Trent Richardson") was acquired by the Colts

* *September 18* – In a bit of a shocking mid-season move the [Browns](/wiki/2013_Cleveland_Browns_season "2013 Cleveland Browns season") traded running back [Trent Richardson](/wiki/Trent_Richardson "Trent Richardson") to the [Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") in exchange for the Colts' first-round pick ([26th overall](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_26 "2014 NFL draft")) in the 2014 draft. The Browns had moved up in the previous year's draft to grab Richardson with the [third overall selection](/wiki/2012_NFL_draft#Pick_3 "2012 NFL draft") and he was presumed to be a cornerstone of the team.[[15]](#cite_note-RichardsonTrade-15)
* *October 2* – Offensive tackle [Eugene Monroe](/wiki/Eugene_Monroe "Eugene Monroe") was traded by the [Jaguars](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season") to the [Ravens](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season") in exchange for fourth- and fifth-round selections in the 2014 draft (picks [#114](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_114 "2014 NFL draft") and [#159](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_159 "2014 NFL draft") overall). The Jaguars had selected Monroe [eight overall](/wiki/2009_NFL_draft#Pick_8 "2009 NFL draft") in the 2009 draft.[[16]](#cite_note-MonroeTrade-16)

### Draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Draft")]

Further information: [2013 NFL draft](/wiki/2013_NFL_draft "2013 NFL draft")

The 2013 NFL draft was held April 25–27, 2013, in New York City.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Prior to the draft the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine"), where draft-eligible players were evaluated by team personnel, was held in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") on February 20–26.[[1]](#cite_note-2013Calendar-1) In the draft, the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") made [Central Michigan University](/wiki/2012_Central_Michigan_Chippewas_football_team "2012 Central Michigan Chippewas football team") offensive tackle [Eric Fisher](/wiki/Eric_Fisher_(American_football) "Eric Fisher (American football)") the [first overall selection](/wiki/List_of_first_overall_National_Football_League_draft_picks "List of first overall National Football League draft picks").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Officiating changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Officiating changes")]

[Dean Blandino](/wiki/Dean_Blandino "Dean Blandino") was named as the league's new Vice President of Officiating, succeeding Carl Johnson.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Referee [Alberto Riveron](/wiki/Alberto_Riveron "Alberto Riveron") was then promoted to the league's Senior Director of Officiating, a newly created position as a second-in-command under Blandino.[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Bill Vinovich](/wiki/Bill_Vinovich "Bill Vinovich"), who worked the previous season as a substitute referee, was then assigned to head Riveron's former on-field officiating crew.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following rule changes were approved at the NFL owners' meeting on March 20, 2013:[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

* The "no-challenge" rule adopted prior to the [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season") season was modified to eliminate the automatic "no-review" penalty when a coach challenges a play that is subject to automatic review by the replay booth (turnovers, scoring plays, and any play inside of the two-minute warning). This change was prompted after last season's [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") game when [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2012_Detroit_Lions_season "2012 Detroit Lions season")' head coach [Jim Schwartz](/wiki/Jim_Schwartz "Jim Schwartz") threw a challenge flag on a play where replay clearly showed [Houston Texans](/wiki/2012_Houston_Texans_season "2012 Houston Texans season")' running back [Justin Forsett](/wiki/Justin_Forsett "Justin Forsett")'s knee touched the ground, but was able to get up and score a touchdown. Due to the way the rule was written at the time the penalty for the errant challenge prevented the play from being reviewed.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Under the revised rule teams will be charged a time-out (or an unsportsmanlike conduct penalty if the team is out of time-outs) when a coach throws a challenge flag on a booth-reviewable play, but the play will still be reviewed if the replay booth believes a review is necessary.[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22) This change has been referred to as the "Jim Schwartz rule".[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* Making it a fumble when a player loses possession of the football while in the act of trying to bring it back to his body. This abolishes the so-called ["tuck rule"](/wiki/Tuck_rule_(American_football) "Tuck rule (American football)") which was adopted prior to the [1999](/wiki/1999_NFL_season "1999 NFL season") season. Under the revised rule it will be ruled a fumble when a quarterback loses possession of the ball after a pump fake while bringing the ball back to his body.[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)
* Tight ends and [H-backs](/wiki/H-back "H-back") will now be permitted to wear uniform numbers 40–49 as well as 80–89. Previously the two positions were treated separately with tight ends allowed to wear only 80–89 and H-backs limited to wearing 40–49.[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)
* "Peel-back" blocks will now result in 15-yard personal foul penalties anywhere on the field. Previously, these types of blocks were permitted within the [tackle box](/wiki/Tackle_box_(American_football) "Tackle box (American football)").
* On field goal and extra point attempts, long snappers will now be considered defenseless players. In addition, defensive players are prohibited from blocking low at the snap of a scrimmage kick.[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)
* For field goal and extra point attempts the defensive team can have no more than six players on either side of the ball at the snap (5 yard penalty), and players cannot push teammates into blockers (15 yard penalty).[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)
* Any player at least three yards downfield or outside of the "tackle box" who leads with his helmet on a hit will be penalized 15 yards for unnecessary roughness. If both offensive and defensive players lead with helmets on the same play, both will be penalized.[[22]](#cite_note-2013Rules-22)

Player safety changes

* Upon recommendation by the league's Head, Neck and Spine safety committee, the NFL notified teams in August that clubs would no longer be allowed to use alternate helmets for [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") or [third jerseys](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") as they had been allowed to do since throwbacks were introduced in [1994](/wiki/1994_NFL_season "1994 NFL season").[[26]](#cite_note-profootballtalk20130917-26) Once players start the season with properly fitted helmets that they are comfortable wearing, the league's Head, Neck, and Spine Committee recommended that players should not switch helmets in mid-season, especially to ones that have not been broken in yet.[[26]](#cite_note-profootballtalk20130917-26) To comply with these new rules, teams will be allowed to change or remove the decals on their regular helmets for such uniforms.[[27]](#cite_note-throwbackban-27)
* All players (except for punters and kickers) were required to wear thigh and knee pads. It was previously mandatory to wear these pads from [1979](/wiki/1979_NFL_season "1979 NFL season") through [1994](/wiki/1994_NFL_season "1994 NFL season").[[28]](#cite_note-KickoffGuide-28)

## Preseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preseason")]

[Training camps](/wiki/Training_camp_(National_Football_League) "Training camp (National Football League)") for the 2013 season opened in late July. The [Buccaneers](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") camp was the first to open with [rookies](/wiki/Rookie "Rookie") reporting on July 17. The Cowboys were the first to open camp to veteran players on July 20. All teams were in camp by July 27.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Prior to the start of the regular season, each team played at least four preseason [exhibition games](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game"). The [preseason](/wiki/National_Football_League_preseason "National Football League preseason") schedule got underway with the [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") on Sunday, August 4. The Hall of Fame game is a traditional part of the annual [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") induction weekend celebrating new Hall of Fame members. It was played at [Fawcett Stadium](/wiki/Fawcett_Stadium "Fawcett Stadium") in [Canton, Ohio](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio"), which is located adjacent to the Hall of Fame building. In the game, which was televised nationally on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season") defeated the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2013_Miami_Dolphins_season "2013 Miami Dolphins season") 24–20.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The 2013 Hall of Fame class of [Larry Allen](/wiki/Larry_Allen "Larry Allen"), [Cris Carter](/wiki/Cris_Carter "Cris Carter"), [Curley Culp](/wiki/Curley_Culp "Curley Culp"), [Jonathan Ogden](/wiki/Jonathan_Ogden "Jonathan Ogden"), [Bill Parcells](/wiki/Bill_Parcells "Bill Parcells"), [Dave Robinson](/wiki/Dave_Robinson_(American_football) "Dave Robinson (American football)") and [Warren Sapp](/wiki/Warren_Sapp "Warren Sapp") was honored during the game.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The 65-game preseason schedule concluded on Thursday, August 29.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Regular season")]

The 2013 season featured 256 games played out over a seventeen-week schedule which began on the Thursday night following [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day"). Each of the league's 32 teams played a 16-game schedule which included one [bye week](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") for each team between weeks four and twelve. The slate featured seventeen games on [Monday night](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") including a [doubleheader](/wiki/Doubleheader_(television)#National_Football_League "Doubleheader (television)") in the season's opening week. There were also seventeen games played on Thursday, including the [National Football League Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") in [prime time](/wiki/Prime_time "Prime time") on September 5 and three games on [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day"). The regular season wrapped up with a full slate of 16 games on Sunday, December 29, all of which were intra-divisional matchups.[[33]](#cite_note-2013ScheduleAnnouncement-33)

Scheduling formula

Under the [NFL's scheduling formula](/wiki/NFL_scheduling_formula "NFL scheduling formula"), each team played each of the other three teams in their own division twice. In addition, a team played against all four teams in one other division from each conference. The final two games on a team's schedule were against the two teams in the team's own conference in the divisions the team was not set to play who finished the previous season in the same rank in their division (e.g. the team which finished first in its division the previous season would play each other team in their conference that also finished first in its respective division). The pre-set division pairings for 2013 were as follows:[[34]](#cite_note-2013Record&FactBook-34)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Intra-conference**  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East")  [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West")  [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")  [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") | **Inter-conference**  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")  [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")  [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")  [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") |

The 2013 regular season schedule was released on April 18, 2013.[[35]](#cite_note-2013_Schedule-35)

Regular season highlights

The 2013 [regular season](/wiki/National_Football_League_regular_season "National Football League regular season") began on Thursday, September 5, with the [NFL Kickoff Game](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff_Game "NFL Kickoff Game") in which the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") hosted the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season"). The game was a rematch of a [two-overtime playoff game](/wiki/Mile_High_Miracle "Mile High Miracle") of the [previous season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NFL_playoffs "2012–13 NFL playoffs") and broadcast on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"). The Ravens, as the reigning [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII") champions, would normally have hosted the kickoff game, however, a scheduling conflict with their [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") counterparts, the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles"), forced the Ravens to start the season on the road (the Ravens' and Orioles' respective stadiums share parking facilities).[[36]](#cite_note-36) The Ravens became the first Super Bowl winner since [2003](/wiki/2003_NFL_season "2003 NFL season") to open their title defense on the road.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The Broncos defeated the Ravens 49–27 on the strength of a record-setting performance by quarterback [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"). Manning completed 27 of 42 pass attempts for 462 yards and seven touchdowns. Manning set or tied numerous league records in the game including most touchdown passes in a game (tied with five others) and records for most career games with at least six, five and four touchdown passes.[[38]](#cite_note-ManningWk1-38)

There were two [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") games held at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in London. On September 29, the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") defeated the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2013_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2013 Pittsburgh Steelers season") 34–27. On October 27, the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") defeated the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season") 42–10. This season was the first of a four-year agreement for the Jaguars to play a home game in London (an agreement which was later extended an additional four years).

The [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2013_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2013 San Diego Chargers season") and [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Raiders_season "2013 Oakland Raiders season") played an unusual late night game in the season's fifth week on October 6. The game, originally scheduled to start at 1:25 pm [PDT](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone"), had to be moved to the evening to accommodate stadium schedules – [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics"), co-tenants of [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum"), had hosted the Game 2 of the [2013 American League Division Series](/wiki/2013_American_League_Division_Series "2013 American League Division Series") the previous night and stadium crews needed nearly 24 hours to convert the stadium from a baseball to a football configuration.[[39]](#cite_note-39) O.co Coliseum was only [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") which hosted both an NFL and an MLB team in 2013.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Although the stadium conversion was complete by 3:30 pm local time, an 8:36 pm kickoff was necessary to avoid conflict with [NBC's *Sunday Night Football*](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), where the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") hosted the [Houston Texans](/wiki/2013_Houston_Texans_season "2013 Houston Texans season") at [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") across the [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay"). The Chargers-Raiders game was the latest start time for a game in NFL history and was broadcast nationwide on the [NFL Network](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The league's traditional slate of [Thanksgiving Day games](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") was played on Thursday, November 28. The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2013_Detroit_Lions_season "2013 Detroit Lions season") hosted the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2013_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2013 Green Bay Packers season") in the early game at 12:30 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"), marking the Packers' 21st Thanksgiving game in Detroit. The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Raiders_season "2013 Oakland Raiders season") visited the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season") in the late afternoon game at 3:30 pm [CST](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone "Central Time Zone"). The evening game featured the defending Super Bowl champion [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season") hosting their [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") rival [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2013_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2013 Pittsburgh Steelers season") at 8:30 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone").[[35]](#cite_note-2013_Schedule-35)

The [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2013_Buffalo_Bills_season "2013 Buffalo Bills season") hosted the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2013_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2013 Atlanta Falcons season") in [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Canada on December 1. The game was played at [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre") one week after the [101st Grey Cup](/wiki/101st_Grey_Cup "101st Grey Cup") ended the [2013 Canadian Football League season](/wiki/2013_CFL_season "2013 CFL season"). On January 9, the Bills and [Rogers Communications](/wiki/Rogers_Communications "Rogers Communications") had announced a five-year extension of the [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### In-season scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In-season scheduling changes")]

The following regular season games were moved either by way of [flexible scheduling](/wiki/NFL_on_Television#Flexible_scheduling "NFL on Television"), severe weather, or for other reasons:

* **Week 5:** The [San Diego](/wiki/2013_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2013 San Diego Chargers season")–[Oakland](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Raiders_season "2013 Oakland Raiders season") game was moved from 4:25 p.m. [ET](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") to 11:35 p.m. ET. The Raiders' [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") counterparts, the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics"), hosted Game 2 of the [2013 American League Division Series](/wiki/2013_American_League_Division_Series "2013 American League Division Series") on the previous night (October 5), and officials at [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum") needed almost 24 hours to convert the stadium from a baseball to a football configuration (O.co Coliseum was the last venue to host both an NFL and an MLB team before the Raiders moved to Las Vegas).[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) The later start time also avoided a conflict with [NBC's *Sunday Night Football*](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), where [San Francisco](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") hosted [Houston](/wiki/2013_Houston_Texans_season "2013 Houston Texans season") at [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") across the [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay") at 8:40 p.m. ET. Additionally, the Chargers–Raiders game was televised on the [NFL Network](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") instead of [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS").[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* **Week 7:** The Houston–[Kansas City](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") game was moved from 1:00 p.m. ET to 4:25 p.m. ET.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* **Week 11**: The Kansas City–[Denver](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") game, originally scheduled as [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS")'s only late 4:05 p.m. ET singleheader game, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET slot on [NBC Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"). CBS originally selected this matchup as one of their "protected games" from flex-scheduling, but later allowed the league to flex it so it could be seen by a national audience.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The original Sunday night contest, the [Green Bay](/wiki/2013_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2013 Green Bay Packers season")–[New York Giants](/wiki/2013_New_York_Giants_season "2013 New York Giants season") game, was then moved back to the 4:25 p.m. ET doubleheader time slot on [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), while the [San Diego](/wiki/2013_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2013 San Diego Chargers season")–[Miami](/wiki/2013_Miami_Dolphins_season "2013 Miami Dolphins season") game was moved from 1:00 p.m. ET to the 4:05 p.m. ET singleheader slot.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* **Week 13:** The Denver–Kansas City game was moved from 1:00 p.m. ET to 4:25 p.m. ET, while the [New England](/wiki/2013_New_England_Patriots_season "2013 New England Patriots season")–Houston game was switched from 4:25 p.m. ET to 1:00 p.m. ET.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
* **Week 14:** The [Carolina](/wiki/2013_Carolina_Panthers_season "2013 Carolina Panthers season")–[New Orleans](/wiki/2013_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2013 New Orleans Saints season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET time slot on NBC. The original Sunday night contest, the [Atlanta](/wiki/2013_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2013 Atlanta Falcons season")–Green Bay game, was then changed to 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox.[[52]](#cite_note-Week14Flex-52) It was the second time the Packers were stripped of a prime-time appearance this season, large in-part due to an injury sustained to [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers").
* **Week 15:** The New Orleans–[St. Louis](/wiki/2013_St._Louis_Rams_season "2013 St. Louis Rams season") and [Arizona](/wiki/2013_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2013 Arizona Cardinals season")–[Tennessee](/wiki/2013_Tennessee_Titans_season "2013 Tennessee Titans season") games were moved from 1:00 p.m. ET to 4:25 p.m. ET.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
* **Week 16:** The [Chicago](/wiki/2013_Chicago_Bears_season "2013 Chicago Bears season")–[Philadelphia](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET on Fox, was flexed into the 8:30 p.m. ET time slot on NBC. Although the original Sunday night contest, the New England–[Baltimore](/wiki/2013_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2013 Baltimore Ravens season") game, featured two playoff contending teams, it was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET to allow CBS to also air a more competitive game.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)
* **Week 17:** The [Philadelphia](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season")–[Dallas](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. ET, was selected as the final NBC Sunday Night Football game, which for the third consecutive season decided the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division champion. The [Buffalo](/wiki/2013_Buffalo_Bills_season "2013 Buffalo Bills season")–New England game was moved to the 4:25 p.m. ET time slot on CBS while the Green Bay–Chicago game (which decided the NFC North division champion) and [Tampa Bay](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season")–New Orleans game were moved to 4:25 p.m. ET on Fox.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2013–14 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NFL_playoffs "2013–14 NFL playoffs")

Wild card round

The wild card round of the playoffs featured the two [wild card](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports)#National_Football_League "Wild card (sports)") playoff qualifiers from each conference being hosted by the two lowest seeded divisional winners. The top two seeds in each conference – the [Seahawks](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season"), [Panthers](/wiki/2013_Carolina_Panthers_season "2013 Carolina Panthers season"), [Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") and [Patriots](/wiki/2013_New_England_Patriots_season "2013 New England Patriots season") – all had first-round [byes](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)"). The games were played January 4–5, 2014.

The weekend's first game on Saturday featured the [Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") staging the second biggest comeback in playoff history to defeat the [Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") by a score of 45–44. The 28-point second half deficit the Colts overcame is exceeded only by the [Bills](/wiki/1992_Buffalo_Bills_season "1992 Buffalo Bills season")–[Oilers](/wiki/1992_Houston_Oilers_season "1992 Houston Oilers season") playoff game from [January 1993](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NFL_playoffs#AFC:_Buffalo_Bills_41,_Houston_Oilers_38_(OT) "1992–93 NFL playoffs") which has become known simply as "[The Comeback](/wiki/The_Comeback_(American_football) "The Comeback (American football)")".[[60]](#cite_note-espn.com-75) It was the first time in *any* NFL game (regular or postseason) that a team won in regulation play (i.e. not overtime) after having trailed by as many as 28 points.[[61]](#cite_note-Elias_WildCard-76) The game was also the highest scoring postseason game to have been decided by a one-point margin[[61]](#cite_note-Elias_WildCard-76) as well as the first game in league history to finish with a 45–44 result.[[62]](#cite_note-77) The Colts and Chiefs combined to gain 1,049 total yards which established a new single-game postseason record, breaking the record of 1,038 yards that was set by the [Bills](/wiki/1995_Buffalo_Bills_season "1995 Buffalo Bills season")–[Dolphins](/wiki/1995_Miami_Dolphins_season "1995 Miami Dolphins season") first-round game on December 30, 1995, and matched in a [Saints](/wiki/2011_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2011 New Orleans Saints season")–[Lions](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Lions_season "2011 Detroit Lions season") first-round matchup on January 7, 2012.[[61]](#cite_note-Elias_WildCard-76) The loss was the Chiefs' eighth straight in the playoffs which broke an NFL record for consecutive playoff losses the franchise had previously shared with the [Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions").[[63]](#cite_note-78)

The [Saints](/wiki/2013_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2013 New Orleans Saints season") beat the [Eagles](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") 26–24 in the Saturday night game. It was the Saints' first ever road playoff victory. The Saints built an early 20–7 lead before the Eagles bounced back to take a 24–23 lead with less than five minutes remaining in the game. However, the Saints worked their way down the field while also working the clock on the game's final drive before [Shayne Graham](/wiki/Shayne_Graham "Shayne Graham") kicked the game-winning field goal from 32 yards out as time expired.[[64]](#cite_note-79)

The early game on Sunday was the only game of the weekend not decided by three or fewer points with the [Chargers](/wiki/2013_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2013 San Diego Chargers season") defeating the [Bengals](/wiki/2013_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2013 Cincinnati Bengals season"), 27–10. Bengals quarterback [Andy Dalton](/wiki/Andy_Dalton "Andy Dalton") committed three second-half turnovers which led to the Chargers scoring 20 unanswered points to overcome a 7–10 halftime deficit. The Bengals loss marks a league record third straight year in which the team has lost its playoff opener, and extended the Bengals' streak of playoff futility to 23 seasons. Every other current NFL team has won a playoff game since the Bengals' last playoff victory in [January 1991](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_NFL_playoffs "1990–91 NFL playoffs").[[65]](#cite_note-80)

In the late afternoon game on Sunday the [49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") defeated the [Packers](/wiki/2013_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2013 Green Bay Packers season") 23–20 on a brutally cold day at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field"). The temperature at game time was just 5 °F (−15 °C) with a [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill") of −10 °F (−23 °C). Quarterback [Colin Kaepernick](/wiki/Colin_Kaepernick "Colin Kaepernick"), who eschewed sleeves and gloves despite the chilly conditions, passed for 227 yards and rushed for 98 more to lead the 49ers to victory in a back-and-forth game. [Phil Dawson](/wiki/Phil_Dawson "Phil Dawson") kicked the winning field goal as time expired. This was the second straight year that the Packers' season had both started and ended with losses to the 49ers.[[66]](#cite_note-81)

Divisional round

The divisional round games were played on January 11–12, 2014 and three of the four were rematches of regular season games – only the Patriots and Colts had not met in 2013.[[67]](#cite_note-82)

In the early game on Saturday, the Seahawks defeated the Saints 23–15. The Seahawks held a 16–0 lead at halftime, but the Saints came back in the second half to make the game interesting. The Seahawks were able to hold on after a late Saints comeback effort, including an [onside kick](/wiki/Onside_kick "Onside kick") recovery, fell short. The Seahawks' offense centered around a 28 carry, 140 yard rushing effort from [Marshawn Lynch](/wiki/Marshawn_Lynch "Marshawn Lynch"), who also scored on a 31-yard run in the fourth quarter.[[68]](#cite_note-83)

Patriots running back [LeGarrette Blount](/wiki/LeGarrette_Blount "LeGarrette Blount") and the Patriots defense were the stars of the Patriots 43–22 victory over the Colts in the Saturday night game. Blount rushed for 166 yards and a franchise-record four touchdowns while Colts quarterback [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") was intercepted four times.[[69]](#cite_note-PatsColtsRecap-84) Patriots quarterback [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") broke a league record for most playoff games for a starting quarterback with 25 (a record he had previously shared with [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre")) and extended his own record of 18 career playoff wins.[[70]](#cite_note-Elias_Division-85) The victory allowed the Patriots to reach the [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game") for the third straight year as well as the eighth time with Brady and head coach [Bill Belichick](/wiki/Bill_Belichick "Bill Belichick").[[69]](#cite_note-PatsColtsRecap-84) Belichick moved into a second-place tie with [Don Shula](/wiki/Don_Shula "Don Shula") on the all-time postseason head coaching wins list, one victory behind [Tom Landry](/wiki/Tom_Landry "Tom Landry").[[71]](#cite_note-86)[[72]](#cite_note-WtLF-ALB-Division-87)

The 49ers defeated the Panthers by a score of 23–10 in the early Sunday game. The 49ers defense twice stopped the Panthers one yard short of the [end zone](/wiki/End_zone "End zone") and also recorded two interceptions and five [quarterback sacks](/wiki/Quarterback_sack "Quarterback sack"). 49ers quarterback [Colin Kaepernick](/wiki/Colin_Kaepernick "Colin Kaepernick") threw for one touchdown and ran for another in the game. The win put the 49ers into their third straight and fifteenth overall conference championship game, matching the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") for most conference championship appearances.[[72]](#cite_note-WtLF-ALB-Division-87) [Jim Harbaugh](/wiki/Jim_Harbaugh "Jim Harbaugh") became the first head coach in NFL history to take his team to the conference championship game in each of his first three seasons.[[73]](#cite_note-88)

In the final game of the divisional round the Broncos beat the Chargers 24–17. It was only the fourth time in the season to date that the Broncos had been held to fewer than 30 points (three of which were against the Chargers), but the Chargers offense could not capitalize.[[74]](#cite_note-89) The Broncos held a 17–0 lead in the fourth quarter before the Chargers launched a comeback that was too little and too late.[[75]](#cite_note-90) The win put the Broncos into the AFC championship game for the first time since the [2005 season](/wiki/2005_Denver_Broncos_season "2005 Denver Broncos season").[[76]](#cite_note-91)

Conference championships

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Tom_Brady_2011.JPG/250px-Tom_Brady_2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tom_Brady_2011.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Peyton_Manning_-_Broncos.jpg/250px-Peyton_Manning_-_Broncos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peyton_Manning_-_Broncos.jpg)

In the AFC Championship Game [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") (left) and [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") (right) met for the 15th time

The conference championships took place on Sunday, January 19, 2014.

The early game featured the Broncos hosting the Patriots in the [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game"). The game was referred to by many as [Manning-Brady XV](/wiki/Brady-Manning_rivalry "Brady-Manning rivalry") as it was the fifteenth meeting (the fourth in the playoffs) of the two starting quarterbacks, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") and [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning").[[77]](#cite_note-92) The Broncos defeated Brady and the Patriots 26–16 behind a 400-yard passing performance by Manning. Manning led the Broncos on two long touchdown drives where each used over seven minutes of game time and were the two longest drives in terms of game time of the Broncos season.[[78]](#cite_note-AFCChampRecap-93) This was Manning's third career postseason game with 400 or more yards passing, equaling [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") for the most such playoff games in league history.[[79]](#cite_note-ESPN_AFC_Champ_recap-94) Broncos head coach [John Fox](/wiki/John_Fox_(American_football) "John Fox (American football)"), who previously led the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2003_Carolina_Panthers_season "2003 Carolina Panthers season") to [Super Bowl XXXVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVIII "Super Bowl XXXVIII") in 2004, became the sixth head coach in NFL history to take two different franchises to the Super Bowl.[[80]](#cite_note-95)

The [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game") had the Seahawks hosting the 49ers in the late game. The Seahawks defense forced three turnovers in the fourth quarter which proved to be the difference in the game. The last of these was a pass intended for [Michael Crabtree](/wiki/Michael_Crabtree "Michael Crabtree") in the endzone which was intercepted by Seahawks linebacker [Malcolm Smith](/wiki/Malcolm_Smith_(American_football) "Malcolm Smith (American football)") off a deflection by cornerback [Richard Sherman](/wiki/Richard_Sherman_(American_football) "Richard Sherman (American football)") with just 22 seconds remaining in the fourth quarter. The interception preserved a 23–17 Seahawks victory.[[81]](#cite_note-96) In an on-field interview immediately after the game with [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") [sideline reporter](/wiki/Sideline_reporter "Sideline reporter") [Erin Andrews](/wiki/Erin_Andrews "Erin Andrews"), Sherman famously directed a rant at Crabtree whom Sherman called a "sorry receiver".[[82]](#cite_note-97)

### Super Bowl XLVIII

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Super Bowl XLVIII")]

[Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") featured the top seeded team from each conference for just the second time in twenty years. The Broncos possessed the league's best offense (in terms of both scoring and yards) while the Seahawks had the league's top defense (also in both scoring and yardage).[[83]](#cite_note-98) The game was played on February 2, 2014, at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), just outside New York City and was televised in the U.S. by [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") with kickoff at 6:32 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"). This was the first ever Super Bowl to be staged outdoors in a cold weather environment[[28]](#cite_note-KickoffGuide-28): 46 although the temperature was a mild 49 degrees at kickoff.[[84]](#cite_note-SB48_recap-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Russell_Wilson%2C_Marshawn_Lynch_with_Lombardi_Trophy.jpg/330px-Russell_Wilson%2C_Marshawn_Lynch_with_Lombardi_Trophy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russell_Wilson,_Marshawn_Lynch_with_Lombardi_Trophy.jpg)

Russell Wilson and Marshawn Lynch with the [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy") at the [CenturyLink Field](/wiki/CenturyLink_Field "CenturyLink Field") in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), February 5, 2014

The game started disastrously for the Broncos who, despite losing the coin toss, received the opening [kickoff](/wiki/Kickoff_(gridiron_football) "Kickoff (gridiron football)"). On the game's first play from [scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage") from the Broncos' 14 yard line, Broncos center [Manny Ramirez](/wiki/Manny_Ramirez_(American_football) "Manny Ramirez (American football)") sent a [shotgun](/wiki/Shotgun_formation "Shotgun formation") [snap](/wiki/Snap_(American_and_Canadian_football) "Snap (American and Canadian football)") over the head of quarterback [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"). The ball traveled into the [endzone](/wiki/Endzone "Endzone") where it was covered by Broncos running back [Knowshon Moreno](/wiki/Knowshon_Moreno "Knowshon Moreno") who was [touched down](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)") for a [safety](/wiki/Safety_(gridiron_football_score) "Safety (gridiron football score)") with just 12 seconds of game time elapsed. This was the quickest score ever in a Super Bowl. The Seahawks did not relinquish the lead in a 43–8 victory.[[84]](#cite_note-SB48_recap-99)

This was the Seahawks first ever league championship since entering the NFL in [1976](/wiki/1976_NFL_season "1976 NFL season"). Seahawks linebacker [Malcolm Smith](/wiki/Malcolm_Smith_(American_football) "Malcolm Smith (American football)"), who scored on a 69-yard interception return plus had a fumble recovery and tallied 10 tackles, was named the game's [Most Valuable Player (MVP)](/wiki/Super_Bowl_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "Super Bowl Most Valuable Player Award").[[84]](#cite_note-SB48_recap-99)

This was the fifth Super Bowl loss for the Broncos, the most for any franchise.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100) Even in defeat, though, Peyton Manning's record-breaking year continued. He set a record for most passes completed in a Super Bowl with 34.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100) He also moved ahead of [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") into first place on the career playoff passing yardage list with 6,589 yards.[[86]](#cite_note-101)[[87]](#cite_note-102)[[88]](#cite_note-103) In addition, Broncos wide receiver [Demaryius Thomas](/wiki/Demaryius_Thomas "Demaryius Thomas") caught 13 passes to set a single-game Super Bowl record.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)

## Pro Bowl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pro Bowl")]

Main article: [2014 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2014_Pro_Bowl "2014 Pro Bowl")

The Pro Bowl is the league's [all-star game](/wiki/All-star_game "All-star game"). The league had raised doubts about the future of the exhibition due to concerns over the game's competitiveness in recent years,[[89]](#cite_note-104) but on March 20, it was announced that the [2014 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2014_Pro_Bowl "2014 Pro Bowl") would indeed take place, receiving a one-year reprieve. As in recent years, the game was held the week before the Super Bowl at [Aloha Stadium](/wiki/Aloha_Stadium "Aloha Stadium") in [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu"), [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"). It was played on Sunday, January 26, and broadcast in the U.S. on [NBC](/wiki/NFL_on_NBC "NFL on NBC").[[90]](#cite_note-ProBowl-105)

The format for the game was considerably altered in an effort to improve competitiveness. The biggest changes included an "unconferenced" format in which players would be selected regardless of the conference in which their team competes, a [draft](/wiki/Draft_(sports) "Draft (sports)") format to select the teams and various tweaks to increase the excitement of the game itself. [Deion Sanders](/wiki/Deion_Sanders "Deion Sanders") and [Jerry Rice](/wiki/Jerry_Rice "Jerry Rice") served as the non-playing captains for the two competing squads.[[90]](#cite_note-ProBowl-105)

## Notable events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notable events")]

Some NFL-related events that made headlines throughout 2013 include:

Harris Poll tabs professional football as America's favorite sport

A nationwide poll conducted by [Harris Interactive](/wiki/Harris_Insights_%26_Analytics "Harris Insights & Analytics") in December 2013 concluded that pro football is the favorite sport of Americans. Of the respondents asked the question, "If you had to choose, which ONE of these sports would you say is your favorite?", 35% chose pro football. That is up by one percentage point over the results of the previous year. Football has taken the top spot in the annual poll each year since it was first conducted in 1985. Baseball finished second, with 14% naming it as their favorite, followed by [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") at 11%.[[91]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Aaron_Hernandez.JPG/250px-Aaron_Hernandez.JPG)](/wiki/File:Aaron_Hernandez.JPG)

Former Patriots tight end [Aaron Hernandez](/wiki/Aaron_Hernandez "Aaron Hernandez")

Aaron Hernandez charged with murder

In August, former [Patriots](/wiki/2012_New_England_Patriots_season "2012 New England Patriots season") tight end [Aaron Hernandez](/wiki/Aaron_Hernandez "Aaron Hernandez") was charged with the [murder of Odin Lloyd](/wiki/Murder_of_Odin_Lloyd "Murder of Odin Lloyd").[[92]](#cite_note-107) Hernandez was released by the Patriots following his arrest in the murder investigation in June.[[93]](#cite_note-108) In an effort to further distance themselves from the troubled Hernandez, the Patriots offered fans an opportunity to trade-in Hernandez jerseys for another jersey of comparable value.[[94]](#cite_note-109) Hernandez would be convicted of the murder charge and sentenced to life imprisonment in April 2015.[[95]](#cite_note-110)

Riley Cooper incident

[Eagles](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") wide receiver [Riley Cooper](/wiki/Riley_Cooper "Riley Cooper") was caught on video using a racial slur during a music concert. After the video went [viral](/wiki/Viral_video "Viral video") during the team's training camp, Cooper was briefly sent away from the team to seek counseling. The Eagles also levied an undisclosed fine.[[96]](#cite_note-111)

Concussion litigation brought by former players

In August, just prior to the start of the season, a [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")765,000,000 settlement proposal was announced in a [class-action lawsuit](/wiki/Concussions_in_American_football#Federal_NFL_concussion_litigation "Concussions in American football") brought against the league by former players who contended that the league concealed a link between head injuries sustained by players and traumatic brain injury which may only become apparent later in life.[[97]](#cite_note-112)[[98]](#cite_note-113) The judge in the case later rejected the settlement on the grounds that the amount may not be large enough to cover the needs of all the plaintiffs.[[99]](#cite_note-114)

Buccaneers release quarterback Josh Freeman in mid-season

In October, the [Buccaneers](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") released quarterback [Josh Freeman](/wiki/Josh_Freeman "Josh Freeman") after trying unsuccessfully to trade him. Freeman had been considered one of the league's best young quarterbacks after leading the Bucs to a 10–6 record in [2010](/wiki/2010_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2010 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season"), but he clashed with head coach [Greg Schiano](/wiki/Greg_Schiano "Greg Schiano") (who took over in [2012](/wiki/2012_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2012 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season")) and was benched earlier in the year. Despite being just 25 years old, Freeman held franchise records for touchdowns and completions and was second in passing yardage. It had come out earlier in the week that Freeman had been in the league's substance abuse program; Freeman described his participation as voluntary and a result of prescription drugs he was taking to treat ADHD.[[100]](#cite_note-FreemanRelease-115) Shortly after his release, Freeman was signed by the [Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season").[[101]](#cite_note-116) Freeman started for the Vikings in week seven, but he posted a passer rating of just 40.6 in that game and did not play another down during the season. He was inactive for nine of the Vikings' final ten games.[[102]](#cite_note-117)

Tampa Bay MRSA outbreak

Three [Buccaneers](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") players – kicker [Lawrence Tynes](/wiki/Lawrence_Tynes "Lawrence Tynes"), guard [Carl Nicks](/wiki/Carl_Nicks_(American_football) "Carl Nicks (American football)") and cornerback [Johnthan Banks](/wiki/Johnthan_Banks "Johnthan Banks") – were diagnosed with [methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*](/wiki/Methicillin-resistant_Staphylococcus_aureus "Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus") (MRSA) infections during the season.[[103]](#cite_note-118) The potentially deadly strain of [staph](/wiki/Staphylococcus_aureus "Staphylococcus aureus") had been encountered by other NFL teams including the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins"), [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") and [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") in previous seasons.[[104]](#cite_note-119) After the third infection was confirmed there was brief discussion as to whether the Bucs' week six home game against the Eagles would be played, but the decision was made to continue with the scheduled game.[[105]](#cite_note-120) The [Falcons](/wiki/2013_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2013 Atlanta Falcons season") even took the step of bringing in a hazardous materials crew to disinfect the visitor's locker room after the Bucs' visited Atlanta in week seven.[[106]](#cite_note-121)

Dolphins bullying scandal

[Dolphins](/wiki/2013_Miami_Dolphins_season "2013 Miami Dolphins season") offensive lineman [Richie Incognito](/wiki/Richie_Incognito#Bullying_scandal "Richie Incognito") was suspended by the team in November after allegations surfaced that he [bullied](/wiki/Bullying "Bullying") fellow lineman [Jonathan Martin](/wiki/Jonathan_Martin_(American_football) "Jonathan Martin (American football)") who left the team earlier in the season due to the impact of Incognito's actions.[[107]](#cite_note-122)[[108]](#cite_note-WellsReport-123)

The league's official investigation into the matter concluded that Martin and other Dolphins employees had been subjected to a "pattern of harassment" at the hands of Incognito as well as fellow linemen [John Jerry](/wiki/John_Jerry "John Jerry") and [Mike Pouncey](/wiki/Mike_Pouncey "Mike Pouncey"). The 144-page report, written by league-appointed investigator [Ted Wells](/wiki/Ted_Wells "Ted Wells"), called the situation a "classic case of bullying".[[108]](#cite_note-WellsReport-123) The report also implicated Dolphins offensive line coach [Jim Turner](/wiki/Jim_Turner_(American_football_coach) "Jim Turner (American football coach)") in some of the abuse; Turner was fired by the team shortly after the report's release.[[109]](#cite_note-124)

Redskins shut down quarterback Robert Griffin III for season's final three games

[Redskins](/wiki/2013_Washington_Redskins_season "2013 Washington Redskins season") head coach [Mike Shanahan](/wiki/Mike_Shanahan "Mike Shanahan") made a decision to bench the team's franchise quarterback, [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III") for the team's final three games of the 2013 season. Griffin had undergone knee surgery after being injured the [previous season](/wiki/2012_Washington_Redskins_season "2012 Washington Redskins season") in which he was named [NFL Offensive Rookie of the Year](/wiki/NFL_Offensive_Rookie_of_the_Year "NFL Offensive Rookie of the Year") after being selected [second overall](/wiki/2012_NFL_draft#Pick_2 "2012 NFL draft") in the 2012 draft. Griffin was much less productive in his second season. Shanahan stated that the decision to start [Kirk Cousins](/wiki/Kirk_Cousins "Kirk Cousins") over Griffin was made to protect Griffin from sustaining another injury, although there was speculation that Shanahan was unhappy about Griffin's friendly relationship with team owner [Daniel Snyder](/wiki/Daniel_Snyder "Daniel Snyder").[[110]](#cite_note-125) Shanahan was fired after the season ended.[[111]](#cite_note-126)

Seahawks quarterback Russell Wilson leads all players in licensed product sales

[Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") the second-year quarterback of the Super Bowl champion [Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") led all NFL players in terms of total licensed product sales (jerseys, T-shirts, figurines, photos, etc.) made from March 2013 through February 2014. The top six players on the list were quarterbacks, including, in order, [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") (who held the top spot the previous year), [Colin Kaepernick](/wiki/Colin_Kaepernick "Colin Kaepernick"), [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III"), [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") and [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"). The only non-quarterback in the top 10 was Wilson's Seahawks teammate, running back [Marshawn Lynch](/wiki/Marshawn_Lynch "Marshawn Lynch").[[112]](#cite_note-127)

Deacon Jones's death

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Deacon_Jones_in_1971_Brady_Bunch_promo.jpg/250px-Deacon_Jones_in_1971_Brady_Bunch_promo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deacon_Jones_in_1971_Brady_Bunch_promo.jpg)

[Deacon Jones](/wiki/Deacon_Jones "Deacon Jones") in 1971.

Legendary [Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_inductees "List of Pro Football Hall of Fame inductees") defensive lineman [Deacon Jones](/wiki/Deacon_Jones "Deacon Jones") died in June.[[113]](#cite_note-128) Shortly thereafter the league honored Jones' legacy by creating the "Deacon Jones Award" to be given annually to the player who records the most [quarterback sacks](/wiki/Quarterback_sack "Quarterback sack").[[114]](#cite_note-129) [Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") linebacker [Robert Mathis](/wiki/Robert_Mathis "Robert Mathis") was the inaugural winner of the honor, and Trey Hendrickson is the current holder.[[115]](#cite_note-130)

Bud Adams's death

[Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") owner [Kenneth S. "Bud" Adams](/wiki/Bud_Adams "Bud Adams") died in October. He was the only owner the franchise, which began in 1960 as the [Houston Oilers](/wiki/Houston_Oilers "Houston Oilers") in the [AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"), has ever had. Adams was a second-generation oil tycoon who made his home in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), Texas.[[116]](#cite_note-131) The team was inherited in equal parts by the families of Adams's three children with Adams's son-in-law Tommy Smith succeeding Adams as the president and CEO of the franchise.[[117]](#cite_note-132)[[118]](#cite_note-133)
