## Records, milestones, and notable statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Records, milestones, and notable statistics")]

The 2013 season saw a number of league records set, most notably:

* A record 11,985 points were scored during the season, with games averaging 46.8 points, the highest average in NFL history. The previous record of 46.5 points per game was set in [1948](/wiki/1948_NFL_season "1948 NFL season").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* A total of 1,338 total touchdowns were scored, surpassing the league-wide record of 1,297, which occurred in [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* A record 863 field goals were made this season, surpassing the record of 852 set in [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season"). Also, kickers converted a record 86.5% of their field goal attempts breaking the record of 84.5% set during the [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season") season.[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") set the record for most passing yards in a season, with 5,477. The previous record of 5,476 was held by [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* Manning also set the record for most touchdown passes in a season, with 55. The previous record of 50 was held by [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)[[120]](#cite_note-135)[[121]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW16-136)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") set the record for most points in a season, with 606. The previous record of 589 was held by the [2007 New England Patriots](/wiki/2007_New_England_Patriots_season "2007 New England Patriots season").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134) The Broncos outscored the next highest scoring team (the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2013_Chicago_Bears_season "2013 Chicago Bears season")) by 161 points, or more than ten points per game.[[122]](#cite_note-TeamOffense-137)
* Eleven teams scored at least 400 points this season, setting a record. The previous record of nine teams was set in [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season") and [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") set the record for most passing yards in a player's first two seasons, with 8,196. The previous record was held by [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton").[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") became the first team to have five players score at least ten touchdowns in the same season: [Demaryius Thomas](/wiki/Demaryius_Thomas "Demaryius Thomas") (14), [Knowshon Moreno](/wiki/Knowshon_Moreno "Knowshon Moreno") (13), [Julius Thomas](/wiki/Julius_Thomas "Julius Thomas") (12), [Eric Decker](/wiki/Eric_Decker "Eric Decker") (11) and [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker") (10). No team had previously had more than three such players.[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") extended his record for the most division titles for a starting quarterback, with 11.[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)[[121]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW16-136)
* [Andre Johnson](/wiki/Andre_Johnson "Andre Johnson"), of the tied the record for most seasons with at least 100 receptions, with five, sharing the record with [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker").[[123]](#cite_note-ALookBack-W16-138)
* Peyton Manning set a record for most touchdown passes without an interception to start a season with 20.[[119]](#cite_note-SeasonReview-134)
* [John Hekker](/wiki/John_Hekker "John Hekker") of set a single season record with an average of 44.2 net yards per punt, The previous record of 44.0 yards was held by [Andy Lee](/wiki/Andy_Lee_(American_football) "Andy Lee (American football)").[[124]](#cite_note-139)

Week 1

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") tied the record for most passing touchdowns in a game, with seven, sharing the record with five other players.[[38]](#cite_note-ManningWk1-38)[[125]](#cite_note-AFC_PotW_01-140)
* Manning set the record for most career games with at least six touchdown passes, with three. He shared the previous record of two with [George Blanda](/wiki/George_Blanda "George Blanda"), [Charley Johnson](/wiki/Charley_Johnson "Charley Johnson"), and [Y.A. Tittle](/wiki/Y.A._Tittle "Y.A. Tittle").[[125]](#cite_note-AFC_PotW_01-140)

Week 2

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the third player with 60,000 passing yards in a career, joining [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") and [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino"). Manning achieved the mark in his 226th game, making him the fastest to reach the milestone.[[126]](#cite_note-141)

Week 4

* [Blair Walsh](/wiki/Blair_Walsh "Blair Walsh") set the record for most consecutive 50+ yard field goals made without a miss, with 12. He previously shared the record with [Robbie Gould](/wiki/Robbie_Gould "Robbie Gould") and [Tony Zendejas](/wiki/Tony_Zendejas "Tony Zendejas").[[127]](#cite_note-142)[[128]](#cite_note-GouldWalshRecord-143)[[129]](#cite_note-144)
* [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") set the record for most passing yards in a player's first 50 games, with 13,976. The previous record of 13,864 was held by [Kurt Warner](/wiki/Kurt_Warner "Kurt Warner").[[130]](#cite_note-145)

Week 5

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") set the record for most touchdown passes before his first interception of the season, with 20. The previous record of 16 was held by [Milt Plum](/wiki/Milt_Plum "Milt Plum").[[131]](#cite_note-146)[[132]](#cite_note-Week5_WTLF-147) Manning's 20 touchdown passes through five games are also a league record.[[133]](#cite_note-Week5_SFS-148) In addition, the 414 yards passing Manning compiled bring his career total to 61,371 eclipsing [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") for second place on the career passing yardage list behind only [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[133]](#cite_note-Week5_SFS-148)
* [Charles Woodson](/wiki/Charles_Woodson "Charles Woodson") tied the record for most defensive touchdowns in a career, with 13, sharing the [Darren Sharper](/wiki/Darren_Sharper "Darren Sharper") and [Rod Woodson](/wiki/Rod_Woodson "Rod Woodson").[[134]](#cite_note-149)

Week 6

* [Robbie Gould](/wiki/Robbie_Gould "Robbie Gould") tied the record for most consecutive 50+ yard field goals made without a miss, with 12, sharing the record with [Blair Walsh](/wiki/Blair_Walsh "Blair Walsh").[[128]](#cite_note-GouldWalshRecord-143)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") and [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") each started the season 6–0, making this just the second time since the introduction of the division format in [1933](/wiki/1933_NFL_season "1933 NFL season") that two teams from the same division have maintained perfect records through six games. The only other pair of teams to have achieved this feat are the [1934](/wiki/1934_NFL_season "1934 NFL season") [Chicago Bears](/wiki/1934_Chicago_Bears_season "1934 Chicago Bears season") and [Detroit Lions](/wiki/1934_Detroit_Lions_season "1934 Detroit Lions season") which each made it to 10–0.[[135]](#cite_note-7fromSunday_w6-150)
* [Reggie Wayne](/wiki/Reggie_Wayne "Reggie Wayne") caught five passes to become the ninth player to have 1,000 receptions.[[136]](#cite_note-WkThatWas_w6-151)
* The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") became the first team to have two players with 1,000 receptions: Wayne and [Marvin Harrison](/wiki/Marvin_Harrison "Marvin Harrison").[[136]](#cite_note-WkThatWas_w6-151)

Week 7

* [Tony Romo](/wiki/Tony_Romo "Tony Romo") set the record for most passing yards in a quarterback's first 100 starts, with 27,485 .[[137]](#cite_note-7fromSunday_wk7-152)

Week 8

* [Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") kick returner [Cordarrelle Patterson](/wiki/Cordarrelle_Patterson "Cordarrelle Patterson") set a league record for the longest kickoff return at 109 yards. This return also ties the record for longest play of any type.[[138]](#cite_note-PattersonReturn-153)
* [Terrelle Pryor](/wiki/Terrelle_Pryor "Terrelle Pryor") set the record for longest run by a quarterback, with a 93-yard run.[[139]](#cite_note-154)[[140]](#cite_note-7fromSundayW8-155)
* [Larry Fitzgerald](/wiki/Larry_Fitzgerald "Larry Fitzgerald") of the [Cardinals](/wiki/2013_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2013 Arizona Cardinals season") became the youngest player to have 800 receptions, at age 30 years, 57 days.[[140]](#cite_note-7fromSundayW8-155)

Week 9

* [Nick Foles](/wiki/Nick_Foles "Nick Foles") tied the record for most touchdown passes in a game, with seven, sharing the record with six other players. He is the first player to accomplish this feat while attaining a [perfect passer rating](/wiki/Perfect_passer_rating "Perfect passer rating") for the game.[[141]](#cite_note-156)

Week 10

* The [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2013_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2013 New Orleans Saints season") set a record for most first downs in a game, with 40. The previous record of 39 was held by the [1988 New York Jets](/wiki/1988_New_York_Jets_season "1988 New York Jets season").[[142]](#cite_note-SaintsCowboys-157)

Week 11

* [London Fletcher](/wiki/London_Fletcher "London Fletcher") set the record for most consecutive starts by a linebacker, with 209. The previous record of 208 was held by [Derrick Brooks](/wiki/Derrick_Brooks "Derrick Brooks").[[143]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW11-158)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the sixth player in NFL history to reach 4,000 career passes completed.[[144]](#cite_note-159)

Week 12

* [Larry Fitzgerald](/wiki/Larry_Fitzgerald "Larry Fitzgerald") set the record for youngest player to reach 11,000 receiving yards, at age 30 years, 85 days. The previous record of 30 years, 222 days, was held by [Randy Moss](/wiki/Randy_Moss "Randy Moss").[[145]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW12-160)
* Linebacker [Robert Mathis](/wiki/Robert_Mathis "Robert Mathis") of the Colts recorded his 40th strip/[sack](/wiki/Quarterback_sack "Quarterback sack") against the Cardinals. This set a new record, passing [Jason Taylor](/wiki/Jason_Taylor_(American_football) "Jason Taylor (American football)") for most strip/sacks in NFL history.[[146]](#cite_note-161)
* The [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") and [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2013_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2013 Green Bay Packers season") and [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") played to a 26–26 tie. This was the first tie game in which both teams scored in the [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") period.[[147]](#cite_note-162)

Week 13

* [Josh Gordon](/wiki/Josh_Gordon "Josh Gordon") became the first player to have at least 200 receiving yards in two consecutive games.[[148]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW13-163)
* Gordon and [Alshon Jeffery](/wiki/Alshon_Jeffery "Alshon Jeffery") each had at least ten receptions and 200 yards, and two touchdowns. This marked the first time in which two players reached these marks on the same day.[[148]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW13-163)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") set the record for most touchdown passes for a player under the leadership of a single head coach with his 353rd touchdown under [Bill Belichick](/wiki/Bill_Belichick "Bill Belichick"). The previous record of 352 was held by [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") under [Don Shula](/wiki/Don_Shula "Don Shula").[[149]](#cite_note-Wk13_Elias-164)
* [Adam Vinatieri](/wiki/Adam_Vinatieri "Adam Vinatieri") became the second player to score at least 800 points with two different franchises, joining [Morten Andersen](/wiki/Morten_Andersen "Morten Andersen").[[150]](#cite_note-Vin13-165)
* Vinitieri also tied the record for most seasons scoring at least 100 points, with 16, sharing the record with [Jason Elam](/wiki/Jason_Elam "Jason Elam").[[150]](#cite_note-Vin13-165)

Week 14

* [Matt Prater](/wiki/Matt_Prater "Matt Prater") set the record for longest field goal, with a 64-yard kick. The previous record of 63 yards was shared by four players.[[151]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW14-166)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") became the fifth player to reach 50,000 career passing yards. Brees became the fastest to reach the milestone, doing so in 183 games. The previous record of 191 games was held by [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning").
* With his seventh game of the year with four or more touchdown passes, [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") set an NFL record for most such games in a season. He broke the record set in [1984](/wiki/1984_NFL_season "1984 NFL season") by [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") and equaled by Manning himself in [2004](/wiki/2004_NFL_season "2004 NFL season").[[152]](#cite_note-Wk14_Elias-167)

Week 15

* All teams playing on Sunday, December 15, combined to score 763 points, setting a record for most points scored in a single day. The previous record of 759 points occurred on [January 1, 2012](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season").[[153]](#cite_note-Wk15_Elias-168)
* [Jamaal Charles](/wiki/Jamaal_Charles "Jamaal Charles") became the first running back in NFL history with four receiving touchdowns in one game. He also became the first player at any position to have four receiving touchdowns and a rushing touchdown in the same game.[[153]](#cite_note-Wk15_Elias-168)[[154]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW15-169)
* [Tony Gonzalez](/wiki/Tony_Gonzalez "Tony Gonzalez") became the first tight end and the fifth player overall in NFL history to reach 15,000 receiving yards.[[154]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW15-169)
* [Calvin Johnson](/wiki/Calvin_Johnson "Calvin Johnson") became the first player to have 5,000 receiving yards in a three-season span (2011–2013).[[155]](#cite_note-170)

Week 16

* [Luke Kuechly](/wiki/Luke_Kuechly "Luke Kuechly") tied the record for most tackles in a game, with 24 (since tackles became an official stat in [1994](/wiki/1994_NFL_season "1994 NFL season")), sharing the record with [David Harris](/wiki/David_Harris_(American_football) "David Harris (American football)").[[121]](#cite_note-SevenFromSundayW16-136)

Wild card round

* The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") achieved the second-largest comeback win in a postseason game, erasing a 28-point deficit against the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season").[[60]](#cite_note-espn.com-75) It was the first time in any NFL game (regular or postseason) that a team won in regulation play after having trailed by at least 28 points.[[61]](#cite_note-Elias_WildCard-76)
* The Colts and Chiefs set the record record for most combined total yards in a payoff game, with 1,049. The previous record of 1,038 yards was shared by the [1995 Miami Dolphins and Buffalo Bills](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NFL_playoffs#AFC:_Buffalo_Bills_37,_Miami_Dolphins_22 "1995–96 NFL playoffs") and the [2011 Detroit Lions and New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_New_Orleans_Saints_45,_Detroit_Lions_28 "2011–12 NFL playoffs").[[61]](#cite_note-Elias_WildCard-76)
* The Chiefs set a record for the longest losing streak in postseason games, with eight. They shared the previous record of seven with the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions").[[156]](#cite_note-171)

Divisional round

* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") set the record for most postseason starts by a quarterback, with his 25th start. He shared the previous record of 24 with [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[70]](#cite_note-Elias_Division-85)
* [LeGarrette Blount](/wiki/LeGarrette_Blount "LeGarrette Blount") became the first player to rush for at least 125 yards and four touchdowns in a postseason game.[[157]](#cite_note-172)

Conference championship games

* The [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2013_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2013 San Francisco 49ers season") tied the record for most conference championship game appearances, with their 15th appearance. They share the record with the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers").[[72]](#cite_note-WtLF-ALB-Division-87)
* [Jim Harbaugh](/wiki/Jim_Harbaugh "Jim Harbaugh") became the first head coach to take his team to a conference championship game in each of his first three seasons.[[158]](#cite_note-173)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") tied the record for most playoff games with at least 400 passing yards, with three, sharing the record with [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees").[[79]](#cite_note-ESPN_AFC_Champ_recap-94)

Super Bowl XLVIII

* [John Fox](/wiki/John_Fox_(American_football) "John Fox (American football)") became the sixth head coach to lead two different franchises to the Super Bowl. He previously coached the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2003_Carolina_Panthers_season "2003 Carolina Panthers season") in [Super Bowl XXXVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVIII "Super Bowl XXXVIII").[[159]](#cite_note-174)
* The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season") recorded the fastest score in a Super Bowl with a safety 12 seconds into the game. The previous record of 14 seconds was held by the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2006_Chicago_Bears_season "2006 Chicago Bears season") in [Super Bowl XLI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLI "Super Bowl XLI").[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)
* The Seahawks set the record for the longest time holding a lead in a Super Bowl, at 59 minutes, 48 seconds of game time.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") set the record for most Super Bowl losses with five. They shared the record of four with the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), and [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots")[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") set a Super Bowl record for passes completed with 34.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)
* Manning move into first place on the career playoff passing yardage list with 6,589 yards, passing [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady").[[160]](#cite_note-175)[[161]](#cite_note-176)[[162]](#cite_note-177)
* [Demaryius Thomas](/wiki/Demaryius_Thomas "Demaryius Thomas") caught 13 passes to set a single-game Super Bowl record.[[85]](#cite_note-FoxSBRecords-100)

### Team statistical leaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Team statistical leaders")]

Offense[[122]](#cite_note-TeamOffense-137)

* Most points scored: **[Denver](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season")**, 606 points *(37.9 points/game)*
* Fewest points scored: **[Jacksonville](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season")**, 247 points *(15.4 points/game)*
* Most total offense: **Denver**, 7,317 yards *(457.3 yards/game)*
* Least total offense: **[Tampa Bay](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season")**, 4,432 yards *(277 yards/game)*
* Most total passing: **Denver**, 5,444 yards *(340.3 yards/game)*
* Least total passing: **Tampa Bay**, 2,820 yards *(176.3 yards/game)*
* Most rushing: **[Philadelphia](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season")**, 2,566 yards *(160.4 yards/game)*
* Least rushing: **[Atlanta](/wiki/2013_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2013 Atlanta Falcons season")**, 1,247 yards *(77.9 yards/game)*

Defense[[207]](#cite_note-TeamDefense-222)

* Fewest points allowed: **[Seattle](/wiki/2013_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2013 Seattle Seahawks season")**, 231 points *(14.4 points/game)*
* Most points allowed: **[Minnesota](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season")**, 480 *(30 points/game)*
* Fewest total yards allowed (defense): **Seattle**, 4,378 yards *(273.6 yards/game)*
* Most total yards allowed (defense): **[Dallas](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season")**, 6,645 yards *(415.3 yards/game)*
* Fewest passing yards allowed: **Seattle**, 2,752 *(172 yards/game)*
* Most passing yards allowed (defense): **[Philadelphia](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season")**, 4,636 yards *(289.8 yards/game)*
* Fewest rushing yards allowed (defense): **[Arizona](/wiki/2013_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2013 Arizona Cardinals season")**, 1,351 yards *(84.4 yards/game)*
* Most rushing yards allowed (defense): **[Chicago](/wiki/2013_Chicago_Bears_season "2013 Chicago Bears season")**, 2,583 yards *(161.4 yards/game)*

## Head coach/front office changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Head coach/front office changes")]

### Head coach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Head coach")]

Offseason

| Team | 2012 head coach | Reason for leaving | 2013 replacement | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2013_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2013 Arizona Cardinals season") | [Ken Whisenhunt](/wiki/Ken_Whisenhunt "Ken Whisenhunt")[[208]](#cite_note-WhisenhuntFired-223) | Fired | [Bruce Arians](/wiki/Bruce_Arians "Bruce Arians")[[209]](#cite_note-AriansHire-224) | Whisenhunt compiled a 49–53 record (including postseason games) making him the Cardinals' all-time coach with most wins. He led the team to its [first Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII") and had just two losing seasons in his six as head coach, but the team had missed the playoffs the previous three years.[[210]](#cite_note-225) The Cardinals started 4–0 in [2012](/wiki/2012_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2012 Arizona Cardinals season"), but lost 11 of their final 12 games, including a franchise-worst 58–0 defeat to the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2012_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2012 Seattle Seahawks season") in Week 14.[[211]](#cite_note-226) Arians went 9–3 as interim head coach of the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2012_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2012 Indianapolis Colts season") filling in for [Chuck Pagano](/wiki/Chuck_Pagano "Chuck Pagano") who left the team to undergo treatment for cancer. Arians's previous head coaching experience was with [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football") from 1984 through 1988.[[209]](#cite_note-AriansHire-224) |
| [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2013_Buffalo_Bills_season "2013 Buffalo Bills season") | [Chan Gailey](/wiki/Chan_Gailey "Chan Gailey")[[212]](#cite_note-GaileyFired-227) | [Doug Marrone](/wiki/Doug_Marrone "Doug Marrone")[[213]](#cite_note-MarroneHired-228) | The Bills compiled a record of 16–32 (.333) and finished last in their division in each of Gailey's three seasons as head coach.[[212]](#cite_note-GaileyFired-227) Gailey chose not to pursue further coaching after the dismissal and retired.[[214]](#cite_note-229) Marrone had spent the past four seasons as head coach at [Syracuse University](/wiki/2012_Syracuse_Orange_football_team "2012 Syracuse Orange football team"). Prior to that he had been the [offensive coordinator](/wiki/Offensive_coordinator "Offensive coordinator") of the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2008_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2008 New Orleans Saints season") for three years.[[213]](#cite_note-MarroneHired-228) |
| [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2013_Chicago_Bears_season "2013 Chicago Bears season") | [Lovie Smith](/wiki/Lovie_Smith "Lovie Smith")[[215]](#cite_note-NYDaily20121231-230) | [Marc Trestman](/wiki/Marc_Trestman "Marc Trestman")[[216]](#cite_note-TrestmanHired-231) | Smith compiled a record of 84–66 (including postseason games) in nine seasons as head coach of the Bears. In the [2012 season](/wiki/2012_Chicago_Bears_season "2012 Chicago Bears season"), the Bears became the first team since the [1996 Washington Redskins](/wiki/1996_Washington_Redskins_season "1996 Washington Redskins season") to miss the playoffs following a 7–1 start to the season.[[217]](#cite_note-232) Trestman had spent the previous five seasons as head coach of the [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes"). During that tenure, he won back-to-back [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup") championships in [2009](/wiki/89th_Grey_Cup "89th Grey Cup") and [2010](/wiki/90th_Grey_Cup "90th Grey Cup"). He had previously coached for several NFL teams.[[216]](#cite_note-TrestmanHired-231) |

Chudzinski had spent the previous two seasons as offensive coordinator of the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2012_Carolina_Panthers_season "2012 Carolina Panthers season") and had served in the same capacity with the Browns in [2007](/wiki/2007_Cleveland_Browns_season "2007 Cleveland Browns season") and [2008](/wiki/2008_Cleveland_Browns_season "2008 Cleveland Browns season").[[219]](#cite_note-ChudHired-234) |
| [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season") | [Mike Mularkey](/wiki/Mike_Mularkey "Mike Mularkey")[[220]](#cite_note-MularkeyFired-235) | [Gus Bradley](/wiki/Gus_Bradley "Gus Bradley")[[221]](#cite_note-BradleyHired-236) | Mularkey compiled a record of 2–14 (.125) in his only season as head coach of the Jaguars.[[220]](#cite_note-MularkeyFired-235) Bradley was the defensive coordinator for the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2012_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2012 Seattle Seahawks season") the previous three seasons. Prior to that he served as linebackers coach for the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") in [2007](/wiki/2007_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2007 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") and [2008](/wiki/2008_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2008 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season"). He spent the first sixteen years of his coaching career toiling in the [collegiate](/wiki/College_football "College football") [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") and [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship") (previously known as Division I-AA) ranks.[[221]](#cite_note-BradleyHired-236) |
| [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2013_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2013 Kansas City Chiefs season") | [Romeo Crennel](/wiki/Romeo_Crennel "Romeo Crennel")[[222]](#cite_note-CrennelFired-237) | [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid")[[223]](#cite_note-ReidHired-238) | Crennel compiled a record of 4–15 (.211) in just over one season as coach of the Chiefs – he had taken over as interim head coach when [Todd Haley](/wiki/Todd_Haley "Todd Haley") was fired with three games remaining in the [2011 season](/wiki/2011_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2011 Kansas City Chiefs season"). The Chiefs two wins in 2012 were the fewest in team history since 2008.[[222]](#cite_note-CrennelFired-237) Reid had been fired earlier in the offseason after spending the past 14 seasons as head coach of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles").[[223]](#cite_note-ReidHired-238) |
| [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") | [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid")[[224]](#cite_note-ReidFired-239) | [Chip Kelly](/wiki/Chip_Kelly "Chip Kelly")[[225]](#cite_note-KellyHired-240) | Including postseason play, Reid compiled a record of 140–101–1 (.581) in fourteen seasons as head coach of the Eagles. He is the franchise's all-time coach with most wins. The Eagles' 4–12 record in [2012](/wiki/2012_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2012 Philadelphia Eagles season") was the team's worst in Reid's tenure.[[224]](#cite_note-ReidFired-239) Kelly had spent the previous four seasons as head coach at the [University of Oregon](/wiki/2011_Oregon_Ducks_football_team "2011 Oregon Ducks football team") where he had developed a reputation as an offensive innovator in leading the team to four straight [BCS bowl](/wiki/Bowl_Championship_Series "Bowl Championship Series") games. He had previously been linked to the [Browns](/wiki/2013_Cleveland_Browns_season "2013 Cleveland Browns season")' head coaching job before backing out and announcing his intention to remain at Oregon. In-season

The following head coaches were replaced in-season:

| Team | 2013 head coach | Reason for leaving | Interim head coach | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2013_Denver_Broncos_season "2013 Denver Broncos season") | [John Fox](/wiki/John_Fox_(American_football) "John Fox (American football)")[[230]](#cite_note-DelRioInterim-245) | Medical leave (weeks 10–13) | [Jack Del Rio](/wiki/Jack_Del_Rio "Jack Del Rio")[[231]](#cite_note-FoxMedical-246) | Del Rio was named as the Broncos' interim head coach when Fox went on medical leave in mid-season.[[230]](#cite_note-DelRioInterim-245) Fox underwent an [aortic valve](/wiki/Aortic_valve "Aortic valve") replacement after becoming light headed on the golf course during the team's [bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") week on November 2. Fox had been aware of the heart condition, but had hoped to delay surgery until after the season.[[231]](#cite_note-FoxMedical-246) Fox returned as head coach for the team's week 14 game on December 8.[[232]](#cite_note-FoxReturn-247) Del Rio, the Broncos' [defensive coordinator](/wiki/Defensive_coordinator "Defensive coordinator"), had previously been the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2011_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2011 Jacksonville Jaguars season")' head coach from [2003](/wiki/2003_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2003 Jacksonville Jaguars season") through [2011](/wiki/2011_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2011 Jacksonville Jaguars season"). The Broncos went 3–1 under Del Rio. |
| [Houston Texans](/wiki/2013_Houston_Texans_season "2013 Houston Texans season") | [Gary Kubiak](/wiki/Gary_Kubiak "Gary Kubiak")[[233]](#cite_note-KubiakFired-248) | Medical leave (weeks 9–10)  Fired (weeks 15–17) | [Wade Phillips](/wiki/Wade_Phillips "Wade Phillips")[[234]](#cite_note-PhillipsInterim-249) | Kubiak went on medical leave to recover from a [transient ischemic attack](/wiki/Transient_ischemic_attack "Transient ischemic attack") or "mini stroke", after he collapsed at halftime of the Texans' Week 9 loss to the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") on November 3. Phillips took over head coaching duties for the second half of the week 9 game and served as interim head coach the following week.[[235]](#cite_note-250) Kubiak returned for Week 11 although he coached the next two games from the press box rather than the sidelines on the advice of his doctors.[[236]](#cite_note-251) Kubiak was fired on December 6, after a loss to the [Jaguars](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season") on [Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") which was a franchise record eleventh straight defeat.[[233]](#cite_note-KubiakFired-248) Kubiak leaves Houston with a record of 63–66 (.488), including the franchise's only two playoff appearances.  Phillips, the Texans' [defensive coordinator](/wiki/Defensive_coordinator "Defensive coordinator"), has nine seasons of previous head coaching experience with the Denver Broncos, [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") and [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), and has previously served as an interim head coach with the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") and [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints").[[234]](#cite_note-PhillipsInterim-249) |

Keim started as a [scout](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)") in 1999 and worked his way up to become director of college scouting in 2006, then director of player personnel in 2008 and finally vice president for player personnel in 2012. Prior to joining the Cardinals Keim had a brief playing career in the NFL and [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League").[[237]](#cite_note-KeimHire-252) |

The 40-year-old Whaley had been groomed as Nix' replacement since he was hired from the Pittsburgh Steelers in 2010. The team went 22–42 (.344) under Smith including a league-worst 2–14 in the [previous season](/wiki/2012_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2012 Jacksonville Jaguars season").[[249]](#cite_note-264) Among the most often cited draft misses in Smith's career was selecting a punter ([Bryan Anger](/wiki/Bryan_Anger "Bryan Anger")) in the third round of the [2012 draft](/wiki/2012_NFL_draft "2012 NFL draft") just a few selections before the Seattle Seahawks picked quarterback [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson").[[247]](#cite_note-SmithFired-262) Smith was replaced by [David Caldwell](/wiki/David_Caldwell_(American_football_executive) "David Caldwell (American football executive)"). He had a five-year playing career with the Packers in the 1980s.[[251]](#cite_note-DorseyHired-266)[[253]](#cite_note-268) |

He is primarily known as a [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") expert. Prior to the Seahawks, Idzik worked for the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") and [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") in a twenty-year NFL career.[[255]](#cite_note-IdzikHire-270) |

Smith")[[226]](#cite_note-TurnerFired-241) | | [Tom Telesco](/wiki/Tom_Telesco "Tom Telesco")[[256]](#cite_note-TelescoHire-271) | The Chargers fired Smith on the same day they dismissed head coach [Norv Turner](/wiki/Norv_Turner "Norv Turner").[[226]](#cite_note-TurnerFired-241) Smith had been the Chargers GM since [2003](/wiki/2003_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2003 San Diego Chargers season"), compiling a 95–65 (.594) regular-season record with five playoff appearances making his tenure the "most successful 10-year stretch" in team history.[[257]](#cite_note-272)[[258]](#cite_note-273) Smith was replaced as GM by 40-year-old [Tom Telesco](/wiki/Tom_Telesco "Tom Telesco") who had been the vice president of football operations for the [Indianapolis Colts'](/wiki/2012_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2012 Indianapolis Colts season") with whom he had worked for the past 15 years.[[256]](#cite_note-TelescoHire-271) |

## Stadium changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Stadium changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/051207-MPLS-006Metrodome-crop.jpg/250px-051207-MPLS-006Metrodome-crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:051207-MPLS-006Metrodome-crop.jpg)

Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome

2013 marked the final season in which the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") played their home games at the [Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey_Metrodome "Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome"), as the team moved temporarily to [TCF Bank Stadium](/wiki/TCF_Bank_Stadium "TCF Bank Stadium") (home of the [University of Minnesota Golden Gophers](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football")) while their [U.S. Bank Stadium](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Stadium "U.S. Bank Stadium") was built at the same site as the Metrodome. The Vikings played the 2014 and 2015 seasons at TCF Bank Stadium and opened their new stadium for the 2016 season.[[260]](#cite_note-275) The Vikings had called the Metrodome home since it opened in [1982](/wiki/1982_Minnesota_Vikings_season "1982 Minnesota Vikings season").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Candlestick_Park_aerial.jpg/250px-Candlestick_Park_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Candlestick_Park_aerial.jpg)

Candlestick Park

This was also the final season in which the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") played their home games at [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park"), as the team moved into the newly built [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium") located in [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"), for [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season"). The 49ers had played in Candlestick since [1971](/wiki/1971_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1971 San Francisco 49ers season") and the stadium hosted *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* 36 times, including the stadium's farewell game on December 23. No other facility had as many *Monday Night Football* appearances.[[123]](#cite_note-ALookBack-W16-138) With the departure of the 49ers, Candlestick Park was left without any permanent tenants. On February 3, 2013, plans to demolish Candlestick Park were announced and took place after the final 2013 49ers game.[[261]](#cite_note-276)[[262]](#cite_note-277)

Two stadiums received new [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights"): In January 2013, Cleveland Browns Stadium was renamed [FirstEnergy Stadium](/wiki/FirstEnergy_Stadium_(Cleveland) "FirstEnergy Stadium (Cleveland)"). The [FirstEnergy](/wiki/FirstEnergy "FirstEnergy") Corporation, an [energy company](/wiki/Energy_company "Energy company") based in [Akron, Ohio](/wiki/Akron,_Ohio "Akron, Ohio"), agreed to pay the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") [$](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar")6 million per year for 17 years to have its name on the team's stadium.[[263]](#cite_note-278) In July 2013, Cowboys Stadium was re-branded as [AT&T Stadium](/wiki/AT%26T_Stadium "AT&T Stadium"), though terms of the naming rights deal remain undisclosed.[[264]](#cite_note-279)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

Several teams made changes to their uniforms or logos prior to the 2013 season:

* The [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2013_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season") revised their logo[[265]](#cite_note-280) and unveiled a new uniform design.[[266]](#cite_note-281) The new design includes all black home uniforms, white road jerseys and a teal alternate jersey. The new jerseys include "claw marks" on the shoulders and a "JAGS" patch over the heart. The new helmet design features a paint scheme that fades from matte black in the front to gold in the back.[[267]](#cite_note-282)[[268]](#cite_note-283)
* The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2013_Miami_Dolphins_season "2013 Miami Dolphins season") updated their logo and unveiled redesigned uniforms.[[269]](#cite_note-284) The new logo features a more streamlined dolphin which loses the football helmet and the "fierce" facial expression.[[270]](#cite_note-285) The new uniforms keep the same basic color palette, but there is less use of orange. The facemask color on the team's new helmets has been changed from aqua to white.[[271]](#cite_note-VikesDolphinsUniWatch-286)
* The [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2013_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2013 Minnesota Vikings season") introduced a slightly tweaked "Norseman" logo[[272]](#cite_note-287) and unveiled new uniforms.[[273]](#cite_note-288) The colors are barely changed, with a new font for the uniform numbers and asymmetrical stripes on the pants. The new helmets feature matte purple paint with glossy logos and black facemasks.[[271]](#cite_note-VikesDolphinsUniWatch-286)
* The [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2013_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2013 San Diego Chargers season") introduced some relatively minor uniform alterations.[[274]](#cite_note-289) The most noticeable change is the switch to "self-color" collars (where the collars match the jersey body) from the previous contrasting color "neck roll" collar design. There were also some changes to the colors used on the nameplates as well as some changes to the socks. The pants and helmets remain unchanged.[[275]](#cite_note-290)
* The [New York Giants](/wiki/2013_New_York_Giants_season "2013 New York Giants season") introduced a new alternate uniform consisting of new white pants which may be worn with the team's existing blue jerseys in place of the usual grey pants.[[276]](#cite_note-291) The team wore this new alternate uniform on November 10 against the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Raiders_season "2013 Oakland Raiders season") and November 24 against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season").[[277]](#cite_note-292)
* In honor of the fifteenth anniversary of the team's relocation to Nashville, the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2013_Tennessee_Titans_season "2013 Tennessee Titans season") wore their alternate navy blue jerseys in two home games. This marked the first time since [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season") that the Titans wore the navy blue jerseys which were their primary home jerseys in their early Tennessee seasons before they switched to their current light blue jerseys.[[278]](#cite_note-293) The Titans also added a patch commemorating the 15th season in Tennessee to their jerseys.[[28]](#cite_note-KickoffGuide-28): 63
* The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2013_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2013 Indianapolis Colts season") added a jersey patch which celebrates the team's 30th season in Indianapolis.[[28]](#cite_note-KickoffGuide-28): 63

Due to a new recommendation that a player should use the same helmet for all games, several teams were forced to make changes in their plans to use alternate and throwback jerseys, including the following:

* The [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2013_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2013 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") shelved their "Creamsicle" throwback uniforms completely.[[27]](#cite_note-throwbackban-27) The [Patriots](/wiki/2013_New_England_Patriots_season "2013 New England Patriots season") similarly chose not to wear their throwback uniforms at all in 2013.[[279]](#cite_note-294)
* The [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2013_Washington_Redskins_season "2013 Washington Redskins season") wore their current helmets (with the center stripe decal removed) along with their throwback uniforms which feature a different shade of burgundy.[[280]](#cite_note-295)
* The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2013_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2013 Green Bay Packers season") also wore their current helmets with their throwback uniforms, but with the stripes and logo decals removed.[[281]](#cite_note-296)
* The [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2013_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2013 Dallas Cowboys season") decided to wear their blue "away" jerseys at their [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") home game, something they have not done since the 1960s. Traditionally the team has worn their throwback jerseys (which include a white helmet) for the Thanksgiving game.[[282]](#cite_note-297)

## U.S. television coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2013_NFL_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: U.S. television coverage")]

This was the eighth and final year of the television contracts with [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS"), [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), and [ESPN](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") before the new nine-year contracts began in 2014. CBS and Fox continued to carry the Sunday afternoon AFC and NFC packages, respectively. NBC carried [Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), the [kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"), and the prime-time [Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") game; and ESPN aired seventeen [Monday Night](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") games in sixteen weeks.[[283]](#cite_note-Newsday_NewTVContract-298)
